title: Last Minute Biology EOC Cram Session // 25min Crash Bio Review!
body: Her over here covers my entire school year with 25 mins, yet she teaches better than my bio teacher 😭, not reading off of the notes or doing any fill in the blank stuff. Anyone else here the day before the test, or taking the STAAR EOC tmrw?
author: @VinaySprints

title: Last Minute Biology EOC Cram Session // 25min Crash Bio Review!
body:
Heres the AI version of my message😭

I took my Florida EOC today and scored a 5. There were 51 questions on my exam. Here's an overview of the topics covered:

Few questions on Embryology
Many questions on ecology (environment and ecosystems)
1 or 2 questions on energy in ecosystems/energy pyramid
3 or 4 questions on organelles
1 or 2 questions on parts of the brain
1 question on viscosity of blood
Many questions on cells & cell theory
Few questions on cellular respiration and photosynthesis
2 or 3 questions on monomers, polymers & macromolecules (like lipids, amino acids, monosaccharides, carbohydrates, proteins, glucose, starch)
1 question on identifying a macromolecule, monomer, or polymer based on an image
Many questions on evolution
1 question on Punnett squares
2 or 3 questions on genetics & inheritance (other than Punnett square question)
1 or 2 questions on Taxonomy, classification, & domains
1 or 2 questions on codons in relation to DNA & RNA
2 or 3 questions on natural selection & other biological processes
1 question on a food web with a diagram
3 or 4 questions on understanding scientific theories and laws
1 question on microscopes (specifically viewing cells via microscopes)
2 or 3 questions on the carbon cycle & sustainability
Few questions on plants (reproduction, genetics, etc)
1 question on properties of water
Some questions were common sense. It's important to eliminate answers you know are wrong and to read the entire question and answers carefully. Words like "most likely" or "best" in relation to the answer choices may indicate that the answer is not 100% correct but is the most plausible based on scientific principles.

When answering questions, even if you feel like you totally understand it, find key words and define them.

- Mitosis, for example, is a type of cell division that creates body cells in animals (including humans) and is involved in growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction in some organisms. It creates two identical diploid cells with the exact same DNA as the parent cell. During mitosis, cells spend most of their time preparing for division and less time actually dividing. The process involves the stages of prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, followed by cytokinesis;

- Fungi are heterotrophic organisms that have cytoplasm, cell walls, and are eukaryotic. They are decomposers, breaking down organic matter;

- Bacteria can be autotrophic or heterotrophic and most have cell walls. They are prokaryotic, unicellular organisms with ribosomes. Mutations in bacteria can help them develop immunity/resistance against antibiotics, similar to how animals evolve through evolution.

It's important to understand the relationships between concepts in the questions and how to interpret diagrams based on that. A testing strategy that helped me was to eliminate choices I knew were wrong and select the best answer, flagging the question to come back to it later. Going back and double-checking can help clarify any questions you struggled with. MOST IMPORTANTLY READ THE QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS CAEFULLY TO AVOID OVERLOOKING IMPORTANT INFORMATION OR WORDS THAT SWIFT THE MEANING OF THE STATEMENT!

I hope this helps! Good luck to everyone taking the exam.❤
author: @AveryUsoro

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: This is true for ALL majors at a university. The purpose of undergraduate studies is to prepare you for graduate studies, not the job market. My recommendation is that most students should just major in math at a university, learn the theories. Then learn job skills like programming, databases, web development, cloud computing, dev ops, and even machine learning on your own.

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Computer Science as a major is not there to get a job in Software Engineering, it was you who was mistaken. It's not that the major is useless. If you want to become a researcher in compilers or graphics theory, of course, the classes are a huge advantage. Telling people not to get a bachelor's is very irresponsible of you. Especially as an educated person from Georgia Tech, btw, I'm also a Georgia Tech grad. CS gives you a platform of knowledge; it's not a way to learn how to do Web Apps, you can do that in 3 months.

author: @Astra060

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Great advice, really.  I have worked as a software engineer, mostly in the embedded field, for the last 15 years.  My original major was in industrial engineering and I picked up a second degree in electrical engineering.  I had to learn the kinds of problems I wanted to help solve in order to find a lasting career, and developing software solutions has always seemed like the best bet.  However, even in the 1990s and the 2000s, I never considered taking computer science; because, as you mentioned, it delved into a lot of theory that did not seem useful in the real world - for what I seem to lean toward.  This can happened with any major, though, and much of the perception of "useful" information (or not) depends on one's own goals.  I have always loved the interaction between digital circuits, software, and real world systems, so now I work as a controls software engineer.......  On the other hand, AI is very interesting to me, where it applies to robotics and real world systems, so I am thinking about learning more about AI through free online courses, or wherever else.  A lifetime of continuous learning is the ticket, you know.
author: @chrisanderson5046

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body: Agree with each word. Took Discrete math, CS and programming at college. They had niothing to do with real life except labs in Python but you can learn that at preety much any 10 dollar course oon udemy.
author: @LizaT77

title: My Honest Advice to Computer Science Majors
body:
I didn't study in the US but in Italy, where the approach is much more theoretical. I felt just like you about theory vs practice until my classmate and I discovered on the job that those with the strongest theoretical knowledge were also the ones who could write better solutions and are the ones who are scaling the career ladder much faster.

The problem is that if you say that you forgot something right after the exam, it means not that the system is broken but that you don't know how to study in a university context. Of course I would have appreciated more practice with more scaffolding but not in spite of theory but together with theory
author: @meMetallo

I love how you described our area in almost all points but the reality is that our state is degrading and residents are being priced out of paradise. Track home communities are destroying wetlands and the wooded areas, pushing all wildlife out. The community we once had is no longer due to all of the growth you stated.

title: What THEY'RE NOT TELLING YOU About Moving To Florida | Honest Review 5 Years Later
body: I have lived in Florida my whole life.  Beware that it's difficult to get home owners insurance on old mobile homes.  I grew up in the Ft.  Lauderdale area and moved at age 33. Yes, anyplace near the coasts are a little cooler than the inland areas. I have lived in north Florida for my last almost thirty years. It's a totally different environment. Growing fruits and vegetables is different.  As far as hurricanes.  Prepare, prepare, prepare.  Evacuate if you need to.  Or build a strong structure in the center line of the state.  Most water is nasty.  But I have well water here in North Central Florida and my well hit a spring line.  So I have spring water. The water hard water with a high amount of calcium.  The salt water coming off the coast will rust your home and car if you live within ten miles of the coast.The environment is hard on everything.  If you don't use your air-conditioner with the humidifier, things in your home will mold.

title: What THEY'RE NOT TELLING YOU About Moving To Florida | Honest Review 5 Years Later
body:
We moved down last July from NJ. We're in Polk County, right next door to Tampa. The heat did take some getting used to, but it can be done. What I loved is that we never turned on the heat this past winter. My son wore pants to school one day the entire year. It was shorts the rest of the time. But not having to wear even a light jacket in the winter, while my co-workers were shoveling snow makes up for a little heat. There have been many days so far this summer where it's been hotter back up there than down here.

Traffic lights - whoever set up these lights needs to be tarred and feathered. And there's very little straight line connection, you need to make lots of turns on the grid and sit through multiple lights.

Roads - So much better than NJ, but just this past weekend, my wife managed to find the 1 pothole, and cracked two rims and blew both tires. It was filled in the next day.

Bugs - one trick I brought with me was giving the dragonflies a place to sit, right next to our deck. Dragonflies eat many times their body weight in smaller bugs. We live next to a fairly sizeable pond, but the bamboo stakes we put in the ground near the house attracted plenty of dragonflies, and as a result we had only 1 mosquito bit all last summer. Our neighbors put them up, as well, and our neighborhood has been pretty much bug free.

Water - yep, it sucks. 'cold' water is 80+ degrees in summer. We ended up getting an extra ice machine to keep up with family demand. We also have 2 high capacity brita filters which work very well.

Schools - not touched on in this video, but Florida has a very open School Choice program. They also have specialized academies within the schools, starting in middle school. Our son is in the aerospace, engineering and robotics academy at his middle school. There are programs for pretty much everything. If college isn't something your kid wants, they can do one of the trade academies, and in most cases graduate with the certifications they need to start working.

Floods - we're inland a bit, so floods aren't an issue for the house, but the streets do sometimes get temporarily blocked after a few downpours.

We had done a scouting trip the previous year to look for good neighborhoods, and to do things like compare grocery and gas prices. Groceries, compared to NJ, were similar. Some things were more expensive, some less, but the average grocery bill was only a couple dollars less. Gas was about 25 cents more a gallon, but I've seen them swing more than 50 cents a gallon each way in only a matter of weeks down here.

title: Brutally honest : MBBS in Georgia 🇬🇪 | MBBS Abroad
body:
college fees in georgia. 5LAKHS.
monthly expenses min. 40k a month. yearly 5 lakhs
flight ticket. insurance. local fines. 1lakhs.

and everything changes as per currency exchange. 1gel =36inr. should calculate as 37

for 6 years come around min. 66 lakhs.

instead choose studying in india. even in private college. which is an safest option to take. with secure degree. as i know there private college in india where the fees is around 12lakhs or 15lakhs per year. for 5 years. which is an best option. if you get a seat.

being in india is totally a bless to have. dont get trapped in abroad mbbs with uncertainty. loneliness. racism. language barrier. trust issues. home sickness.

and FMGE make it harder. it not about clearing exam. its about local politics in hospital evironment after clearing everything.

for students: you cant get freedom after going to abroad. instead you will get to know how to survive on your own. to take care of yourself. A life will more responsibility and survival mode.

INDIA: they focus on how to study. what to study.
ABROAD: they manage both studies and money issues plus racism.discrimination. loneliness. and many more. obviously how to COOK.

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body: Get the country & western recordings of Ray Charles, newly restored and remastered, plus a brand new compilation of iconic songs from Ray's country catalog: https://linktr.ee/raycharlesmusic
author: @raycharlestv

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body: My name is Georgia.  My dad's family was from Georgia. I was born in 1941 and Daddy insisted I would be named Georgia!  Many years later, I was a student at Ohio State University in Ohio and a guy dated got tickets to surprise me to see Ray Charles and the Rayettes at the Auditorium. What a show it was!!  One of the best nights of my life..Ray Charles is a master of song and when he sang "Georgia On My Mind" , I had tears in my eyes..so beautiful!  I'll never forget it!
author: @georgiadupuis

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body:
How ironic: a blind man saw the world brighter than 99% of humanity.
Long live legend, your music will keep you alive 😢🎹

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body:
From being banned from Georgia
To creating the official song of Georgia.....Ray you are legend❤️

Context :- In the 1960s, Ray Charles was banned from performing in Georgia after he refused to play a segregated concert. His stance against racial segregation led to him being blacklisted in the state for a time. However, years later, his song "Georgia on My Mind" became the official state song of Georgia in 1979. The ban was eventually lifted, and he was honored by the state, marking a powerful full-circle moment in his legacy.
author: @Unknown_007-o

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body: I am white,British from Liverpool,UK ,and in the 1960's my late father who was a merchant seafarer brought back a stack of Ray Charles and Sam Cooke records from a voyage he made from Liverpool to Jacksonville,Florida. I was seven years old at the time, but these two wonderful singers and their beautiful music have stayed in my heart and mind ever since. Thanks Dad,you had great taste.

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body:
Sitting here listening with my 89 year old father. It may be the last time I see him... He loves beautiful music ❤

EDIT
My Father passed away peacefully on the evening of July 28. I'm so glad we listened to music together. He enjoyed it so much.

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body: I was featured electric bass player in a trio that performed Georgia on My Mind and other Ray Charles songs with Ray Charles in Charleston, West Virginia Symphony Orchestra.  Ray Charles told the entire orchestra during the rehearsal that, the bass player, "me", could really play.  The compliment from him was the thrill of a lifetime for me.  It was cool.  Another 15 minutes of fame.

title: Ray Charles - Georgia On My Mind (Official Video)
body: Ray Charles what a great human being. He was born dirt poor, went blind at 8 years old.  His mother, not able to support him with no money, sent him away.  He learned to play the piano and performed in clubs.  He was booked at a club in Georgia, but refused to go on because it was segregated.  He was a drug addict and kicked the habit cold turkey.  After he cleaned himself up, he was welcomed to Georgia as their fellow Georgian.  He went ont to become a philanthropist donating his money to poor countries.  God rest his soul.

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I did 8 on a 12 for Agg assault w/deadly weapon on a public servant in TDCJ (Estelle, Wynne, Dalhart, Hughes, Ellis, Byrd, Holliday, Montford). I was released in 2016 from a halfway house in Houston, with nothing but some clothes and a Pitbull they'd given me.  Today, eight years later, I have earned Two master's degrees; a Master's in IT and a Master's in CS/Cyber Security Engineering which I just completed. I received my Bachelor's in Cyber Security four years after my release, while working full-time. Now, after prison, I have the life I should have always lived, clean, sober, and FREE.  "CHOICES."
author: @unsilent369

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I love this guy because he never tries to put a narrative spin on these videos. He just lets the interviewees speak for themselves honestly without judgement and encourages positivity. The people know they’ve done bad things but are still treated like human beings while not framing them as victims or heros, just real people with real lives. He’s like a much better version of Tyler Olivera, someone who try’s to push narratives that were predetermined before the filming. It’s good to see someone let people give honest accounts of their own experiences, no matter how varied they may be.
author: @oscarlindahl5662

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I met one of my best friends by going to jail for six months. Shared a room with him for months while we awaited sentencing. I ended up getting moved to a program and on the way out, he gave me a couple bags of coffee and a rack of Ramen to take since I couldn't buy commissary for two weeks. Now we have both been out for over six years and stay in contact, go to baseball games and just check in on each other. I'm grateful for that aspect of jail.
author: @SeanSinha

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: I was in prison for a bit and the amount of guys on their 3rd, 4th, even 6th bid blew my mind. I don’t think I ever talked to a guy that was actually innocent the whole time. I was guilty. Was a 22 month sentence I got parole in 366 days served by taking every class, program, and job available. That was 2018 when I got out and I’m not on probation or anything now. Been sober since 2021. Realized I had never been arrested when I was 100% sober. It’s a choice, 99% of the time it is a choice to break the law and you get what you get. Lost count how many people I met that I was glad they were locked up. There are evil people in this world, believe that. Truly evil, no chance of changing, wanna just burn it all down type people. Be glad there is a prison system for that reason.
author: @portlandGTR

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body:
Back in the 80's when I was in the military, I was wrongfully court-martialed for something I wasn't responsible for. I was given 8 months of confinement. (Yeah, I know. It wasn't much.) When I got to the facility, I was lead through a common area, past barred areas that held a bunch of bunkbeds, all the way to the back, down a small hall. I was placed in a metal box, with bars for a door, a cot, school type desk and that was it. I was allowed reading material, and that was it. Each day, the sergeant that ran the place would come back and see me, and ask how I was doing. I would stand at attention and say "I'm doing fine". After the third day, he asked me again and again, I'd say I was fine. He then said, you know, I don't get you. I asked, why's that sergeant? He said, each day you keep saying you're fine. You never ask to be let out. What's wrong with you? I said, I didn't know I could get out. I thought this was going to be my cell for the next 8 months. So, why fight something I can't change. I've got food, a place to sleep, reading material. That's all I need, so I'm fine. I'm not like depressed, or upset or anything.
He said, and here I thought maybe you were crazy or something. You're not supposed to be fine in here all by yourself. Then he laughed and opened the door. He said, I usually let people out the first day, but you kept saying you were fine.
I had no problem being locked up all by myself. I've always been kind of a loaner, in a way. If something ever happened and I got locked up, I'd rather be in solitary all by myself, than to be around a bunch of hardened criminals that may be looking for a new guy to attack for no reason. Then, one of two things are going to happen. Either I'll kill someone, or they'll kill me. And, I don't like either of those two outcomes.
Oh, and by the way, I appealed my court-martial and won three years later. I got 3 years of backpay including raises, got my rank back, and since I had one year left on my last enlistment, I went back in. Unfortunately, due to the Clinton cut back on the military, I was among many others on base that was told, your services are no longer required. Pissed me off. Spend 3 years fighting to clear my name, get it all back just to have it yanked from me again. At least they paid me to get out.
author: @macD723

title: Inside the Largest Prison in Texas
body: It's great to see the changes that these brothers are making with their lives. I did 25 years for bank robbery in the Georgia Department of Corrections. I have been out since 2005. My first 15 years on the outside. I went back into prison to help other guys to change their life. I've also written five books since I have been out. One of those books is called. " Breaking the Cycle of Recidivism: Gettin' Out and Goin' Straight." Keep up the great work..

title: what you NEED to know about Georgia State University | pt. 1
body:
Honestly the manners thing doesn't surprise me. I'm from California and moved to Georgia last year. I was raised with manners, but in California you never were really friendly to people you didn't know, like smiling at them or even waving. I live in a more rural area so out here the southern mannerisms are common, but then when I go into Atlanta it's like California where it's weird to be friendly.

Also, I'll be doing GSU PC this fall as a first year, so thanks for this video :) I probably will transfer to the main campus for my bachelors because it seems like a good school and that's easier than transferring to another four year.
author: @irenicrose

title: Ranking EVERY Florida College #college #university #collegelife #fsu #uf
body:
UCF’s biggest plus is it’s in Orlando. You’re going to naturally have a good night life and plenty to do. As someone from South Florida it was the perfect distance from family to have some separation while also being close enough that going home for breaks and holidays wasn’t too much of a hassle.

Biggest downside is the massive student body. Parking is a mess and if you’re not a social butterfly it can be intimidating.
author: @bellbilly32

title: My Brutally Honest Opinions on the University of Georgia
body: I lived in the dorms for all 4 years at the University of California, San Diego (UCSD) in beautiful La Jolla, CA.  The campus is huge, but everything I needed was within walking distance from my dorm: cafeteria, cafes, library, classrooms, labs, and even the beach. I always had a single room in the dorms.

title: My Brutally Honest Opinions on the University of Georgia
body: I graduated from UGA in 66 (bachelors degree) and in 68 (masters degree). I have not been back since then. I came across your video and was curious to hear your thoughts. I see as far as parking is concerned, nothing has changed since I went there, probably worse. my solution to the parking problem was, live on campus. I lived in the Joe Brown dorm. For me it was central to everything.
author: @WyomingTraveler

title: My Brutally Honest Opinions on the University of Georgia

author: @PaoloAntoine

title: My Brutally Honest Opinions on the University of Georgia
body: You never know how much you actually liked the dining halls until you graduate, live on your own and realize you have to figure out your meals every day.  😂  Lived in Myers for 4 years and it was a great place to live - one block from the dining hall and 2 blocks from the stadium and 2 or 3 blocks from the Coliseum.  Parking was much better back then before UGA paved over every parking lot to put up a new building.  The people and friends made it a great place to go to school - the admin not so much.  On parking - a friend of mine in grad school (law student) got a ticket outside of Snelling.  He met with the parking director and threatened them with a class action law suit regarding his ticket because the handicap spot in which he received a ticket wasn't properly marked by state code.  No sign and asphalt so messed up you could hardly see the handicap paint.  They let him out of his ticket.  Go Dogs!!!
author: @meadowsproperties5085
